root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

the algebraic theory III: vector bundles

Table of contents

  1. Locally trivial vector bundles
  2. Construction lemmas
  3. Operations on vector bundles
  4. Cotangent and normal bundles
  5. Some fundamental natural isomorphisms
  6. Tensor fields

Prerequisites

In this post, we continue to assume that our manifolds are embedded in an ambient euclidean space, in the style of Guillemin and Pollack’s book.

Locally trivial vector bundles

If $M$ is a manifold, we saw in the previous post that the tangent bundle $T(M)$ is locally trivial, in the sense that $M$ is covered by open sets $V$ such that there is a local trivialization

\begin{equation}\notag \alpha: V \times \bbr^n \to \pi^{-1}(V) = T(V). \end{equation}

Here, $\pi$ is the natural smooth projection map $\pi:T(M) \to M$, and to say that $\alpha$ is a local trivialization means that it is a diffeomorphism of smooth manifolds which restricts to a linear isomorphism on each fiber. The product space $V\times \bbr^n$ is given the trivial basic vector bundle structure with base $M$, where the natural projection $\pi_1: V \times \bbr^n \to V$ is simply the projection of the product onto the first cartesian factor.

These considerations inspire the following abstract structure:

Definition. Let $E$ be a rank-$k$ basic vector bundle over a smooth manifold $M$ with natural projection $\pi:E \to M$, in the sense of the definition in the post on tangent bundles. We shall say $E$ is a locally trivial vector bundle of rank $k$, or simply a rank-$k$ vector bundle, if $E$ has a smooth manifold structure such that $\pi$ is smooth, and if $M$ is covered by open sets $V$ such that there is a diffeomorphism

\begin{equation}\notag \alpha: V \times \bbr^k \to \pi^{-1}(V) \end{equation}

which is also a homomorphism of basic vector bundles over $V$. Such a diffeomorphism $\alpha$ is called a local trivialization (over $V$).

A homomorphism of (locally trivial) vector bundles is a homomorphism of basic vector bundles which is also smooth; an isomorphism of (locally trivial) vector bundles is an invertible homomorphism of vector bundles whose inverse is also a homomorphism of vector bundles.

One of the most important and useful properties of homomorphisms of vector bundles is that the isomorphism property may be tested locally, on the fibers. Precisely:

Local Criterion for Vector Bundle Isomorphisms. Let $\alpha: E \to E’$ be a homomorphism of rank-$k$ vector bundles over a common base manifold $M$. Then $\alpha$ is an isomorphism of vector bundles if and only if each induced map $\alpha_p:E_p \to E’_p$ on the fibers is a linear isomorphism.

I suggest that you attempt to prove this theorem on your own. You will see that the local triviality of the vector bundles is used in a crucial way. You may then use the criterion to prove:

Topological Criterion for Vector Bundle Isomorphisms. Let $\alpha: E\to E’$ be a homomorphism of rank-$k$ vector bundles over a common base manifold $M$. Then $\alpha$ is an isomorphism of vector bundles if and only if it is a diffeomorphism of smooth manifolds.

Construction lemmas

It will be very important for us to be able to construct vector bundles and homomorphisms between them efficiently. In order to do this, we will first begin by identifying the sorts of necessary structures that come along with vector bundles and homomorphisms, and then show that these structures are actually sufficient to construct such objects.

Let’s begin with homomorphisms. If $E$ and $F$ are two vector bundles (say, of rank $m$ and $n$, respectively), then a homomorphism $\alpha:E \to F$ is at the most basic level just a collection

\begin{equation}\label{hom-eqn} \{\alpha_p: E_p \to F_p\}_{p\in M} \end{equation}

of linear transformations of vector spaces, parametrized by the points of $M$. However, the collection \eqref{hom-eqn} is not completely arbitrary, since the maps must assemble together to yield a smooth map. This is a non-trivial condition on the maps in \eqref{hom-eqn}.

In detail, suppose that we select an open set $V$ of $M$ over which both $E$ and $F$ trivialize. If we let $\pi_E$ and $\pi_F$ be the natural projection maps, then we have local trivializations

\begin{equation}\notag \phi: V \times \bbr^m \to \pi_E^{-1}(V) \quad \text{and} \quad \psi: V\times \bbr^n \to \pi_F^{-1}(V). \end{equation}

Then, the composite map

\begin{equation}\notag V \times \bbr^m \xrightarrow{\phi} \pi_E^{-1}(V) \xrightarrow{\alpha} \pi_F^{-1}(V) \xrightarrow{\psi^{-1}} V \times \bbr^n \end{equation}

is given by

\begin{equation}\notag (p,v) \mapsto \left(p, \left(\psi^{-1}_p \circ \alpha_p \circ \phi_p\right)(v)\right), \end{equation}

which shows that the map

Let $M$ be a manifold. If for each $p\in M$ we have a vector space $E_p$ of fixed dimension $k$, then by setting

\begin{equation}\notag E = \bigsqcup_{p\in M} E_p \end{equation}

we obtain a basic vector bundle over $M$. However, more structure is needed if we want $E$ to form a (locally trivial) vector bundle.

In particular, we need the maps that will serve as local trivializations. So, we suppose that $M$ is covered by open sets $V_i$ for which there are isomorphisms

\begin{equation}\notag \alpha_i: V_i \times \bbr^k \to \pi^{-1}(V_i) \end{equation}

of basic vector bundles. We could then topologize $E$ by equipping it with the coarsest topology for which each of the $\alpha_i$’s is a homeomorphism onto its image, but this isn’t enough to give $E$ a smooth structure. For this, one last condition is required, which is given as part (3.) in the following lemma:

Vector Bundle Construction Lemma. Let $M$ be a manifold, and suppose that for each $p\in M$ we have a vector space $E_p$ of fixed dimension $k$. Set

\begin{equation}\notag E = \bigsqcup_{p\in M} E_p \end{equation}

and let $\pi:E \to M$ be the natural projection. Suppose further that we are given:

  1. An open cover $\{V_i\}$ of $M$.
  2. For each $i$, an isomorphism \begin{equation}\notag \alpha_i: V_i \times \bbr^k \to \pi^{-1}(V_i) \end{equation} of basic vector bundles over $V_i$.
  3. For each pair of overlapping open sets $V_i$ and $V_j$, a smooth map \begin{equation}\notag \tau_{ji}: V_i \cap V_j \to \Aut{\bbr^k} \end{equation} such that homomorphism $\alpha_j^{-1} \circ \alpha_i$ of basic vector bundles (over $V_i \cap V_j$) on the fiber over $p$ is given by \begin{equation}\notag v \mapsto \tau_{ji}(p)(v), \end{equation} for all $p\in V_i \cap V_j$.

Operations on vector bundles

Cotangent and normal bundles
